name: lenet

members.name: LeNetConfig
members.signature: LeNetConfig(num_classes: int = 10, in_channels: int = 1, activation: str = 'tanh', pooling: str = 'avg')

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 10, in_channels: int = 1, activation: str = 'tanh', pooling: str = 'avg')

members.extended:
`activation` controls the nonlinearity:
  - `"tanh"`    — original paper (Gradient-Based Learning, 1998)
  - `"relu"`    — modern convention

`pooling` controls the sub-sampling layers:
  - `"avg"`     — original paper (average pooling / sub-sampling)
  - `"max"`     — modern convention

`in_channels` defaults to 1 (grayscale). Set to 3 for RGB inputs,
though the canonical use-case is MNIST / single-channel images.

members.name: LeNet

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary:
Configure a `DataLoader`; see the class docstring for parameter
semantics.

raises.description: On any of the above mutual-exclusion / range violations.

notes:
`sampler` / `batch_sampler` / `shuffle` / `batch_size` /
`drop_last` interact: passing `batch_sampler` precludes the
other four; passing `sampler` precludes `shuffle`. When no
sampler is supplied, a `SequentialSampler` (`shuffle=False`)
or `RandomSampler` (`shuffle=True`) is constructed
automatically. `persistent_workers` requires `num_workers > 0`.

members.extended:
Implements the canonical convolutional trunk of the original LeNet-5
network from LeCun et al., "Gradient-Based Learning Applied to
Document Recognition", Proc. IEEE 1998: three convolutional layers
(C1, C3, C5) interleaved with two sub-sampling layers (S2, S4).  The
receptive field grows from a single $5\times5$ patch in C1 to
the entire $32\times32$ input by C5 — so for the canonical
input size the C5 output collapses to a $1\times1$ spatial map
of 120 channels, acting as a fully-connected layer in conv form.
Used as the canonical "Hello World" of convolutional networks and
as the smallest paper-cited baseline in the model zoo.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use `lenet_5` for the
paper-cited tanh + average-pool variant; pass a custom config to
switch to modern `activation="relu"` / `pooling="max"` or to
accept RGB input via `in_channels=3`.

examples:
Run a single forward pass on a batch of MNIST-shaped inputs:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.lenet import lenet_5
>>> backbone = lenet_5()
>>> x = lucid.randn(8, 1, 32, 32)
>>> out = backbone(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (B, 120, 1, 1)
(8, 120, 1, 1)
notes:
From LeCun et al., "Gradient-Based Learning Applied to Document
Recognition", Proc. IEEE 86(11):2278–2324, 1998, Figure 2.  Each
convolution computes

$$
h_{i,j}^{(k)} = \phi\!\left(
    \sum_{c}\sum_{u,v} W_{u,v,c}^{(k)} \, x_{i+u,\,j+v,\,c}
    + b^{(k)}
\right),
$$
where $\phi$ is $\tanh$ in the paper.  The original
sub-sampling layer was a *trainable* $2\times2$ average pool
with learnable scale and bias; this implementation uses standard
parameter-free `lucid.nn.AvgPool2d` for compatibility with
modern training recipes.  Total parameter count is approximately
60 k — small enough to train to convergence on MNIST in minutes on
a CPU.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: features
attributes.description:
The C1-S2-C3-S4-C5 convolutional stack — see
`_build_features` for the exact layer chain.

attributes.name: feature_info
attributes.description:
Per-stage descriptor (channels + reduction factor) exposed via
`BackboneMixin` for downstream decoder modules.

members.name: LeNetForImageClassification

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: LeNet-5 with F6 fully-connected layer and final classifier.
members.extended:
Combines a `LeNet` convolutional trunk with the original
paper's two-layer fully-connected head: a hidden layer F6 mapping
120 → 84 followed by the output layer mapping 84 →
`config.num_classes`.  When `labels` are supplied to
`forward`, a cross-entropy loss is returned alongside the
logits; otherwise `loss` is `None`.  Used as a teaching baseline
on MNIST / Fashion-MNIST and as a parity reference for LeNet-style
architectures.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Architecture spec.  Use `lenet_5_cls` for the paper-cited
configuration (10-way classifier, tanh, average pooling); pass a
custom config to retarget `num_classes` or to switch the
activation/pooling family.

examples:
Run inference on a small MNIST-style batch:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.lenet import lenet_5_cls
>>> model = lenet_5_cls()
>>> x = lucid.randn(4, 1, 32, 32)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(4, 10)
>>> out.loss is None
True
Compute a training loss given labels:
>>> labels = lucid.tensor([0, 1, 2, 3], dtype=lucid.int64)
>>> out = model(x, labels=labels)
>>> out.loss.shape
()
notes:
From LeCun et al., "Gradient-Based Learning Applied to Document
Recognition", Proc. IEEE 86(11):2278–2324, 1998, Figure 2.  The
original output layer was a Gaussian-RBF unit comparing F6
activations against fixed digit templates; this implementation uses
the modern linear + cross-entropy head, which gave essentially the
same accuracy in subsequent reimplementations.  Loss is the standard
categorical cross-entropy

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N}
    \log \operatorname{softmax}(\text{logits}_n)_{\,y_n},
$$
computed only when `labels` is not `None`.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: features
attributes.description:
The C1-S2-C3-S4-C5 convolutional stack inherited from the
backbone.

attributes.name: act_f6
attributes.description:
Activation applied after F6 — `lucid.nn.Tanh` in the
paper, `lucid.nn.ReLU` if `config.activation="relu"`.

members.name: lenet_5
members.extended:
Builds a `LeNet` with the paper-cited LeCun 1998 topology:
three convolutions (C1: 1→6, C3: 6→16, C5: 16→120) interleaved with
two $2\times2$ sub-sampling layers, using $\tanh$
activations and parameter-free average pooling.  Approximately
44 k convolutional parameters (≈60 k including the F6 + output
layers of the classifier variant).  Designed for $1\times32 \times32$ grayscale digit inputs.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored — the returned model is randomly initialised.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `LeNetConfig` to
customise individual fields.  Use `activation="relu"` and
`pooling="max"` for the modern ReLU + max-pool reimplementation,
or `in_channels=3` for RGB inputs.

returns.description:
Backbone with the LeNet-5 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.lenet import lenet_5
>>> model = lenet_5()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 1, 32, 32)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (B, 120, 1, 1)
(1, 120, 1, 1)
notes:
See LeCun et al., "Gradient-Based Learning Applied to Document
Recognition", Proc. IEEE 86(11):2278–2324, 1998, Figure 2.  The
canonical metric is MNIST test-set error: the original paper reports
0.95% with the full distortion-augmented training recipe.  No
paper-cited "tiny / large" LeNet variants exist (H11) — pass
`activation`/`pooling` overrides to get the common modernised
flavours.

members.name: lenet_5_cls
members.summary: LeNet-5 image classifier (C1-S2-C3-S4-C5 + F6 + linear head).
members.extended:
Builds a `LeNetForImageClassification` with the paper-cited
LeCun 1998 configuration: three convolutions, two sub-sampling
layers, a hidden F6 layer (120 → 84), and a final linear projection
to `config.num_classes` (default 10 for MNIST).  Approximately
60 k parameters total.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future pretrained-weight loading.  Currently
ignored.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Keyword overrides forwarded into `LeNetConfig` to
retarget `num_classes` (e.g. `num_classes=100` for
CIFAR-100) or to enable the modern ReLU + max-pool variant via
`activation="relu"`, `pooling="max"`.

returns.description:
Classifier with the LeNet-5 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.lenet import lenet_5_cls
>>> model = lenet_5_cls()
>>> x = lucid.randn(2, 1, 32, 32)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(2, 10)
notes:
See LeCun et al., "Gradient-Based Learning Applied to Document
Recognition", Proc. IEEE 86(11):2278–2324, 1998, Figure 2.  The
canonical MNIST test-set error is 0.95% with the original distortion-
augmented training recipe.

citation: LeCun, Yann, et al. "Gradient-based learning applied to document recognition." Proceedings of the IEEE, vol. 86, no. 11, 1998, pp. 2278–2324.
theory:
LeNet-5 is the prototypical convolutional neural network and the
direct ancestor of every modern vision architecture.  Designed for
handwritten-digit recognition on 32×32 grayscale inputs, it
interleaves *learned* $5\times5$ convolutions with fixed
$2\times2$ sub-sampling (average pooling) layers, ending in
two fully-connected layers and a Gaussian-RBF output layer.

The key insight of LeCun et al. was that local receptive fields,
shared weights, and spatial sub-sampling — together — give a model
that is approximately invariant to small translations, scalings,
and distortions of the input.  Convolution enforces *weight
sharing*: the same $5\times5$ filter slides across the image,
so a feature detector learned in one location applies everywhere.
Sub-sampling layers then *pool* responses over a neighbourhood,
yielding coarser feature maps that are robust to the exact position
of each feature.

Formally, a LeNet layer computes



$$
h_{i,j}^{(k)} = \phi\!\left(
    \sum_{c}\sum_{u,v} W_{u,v,c}^{(k)} \, x_{i+u,\,j+v,\,c}
    + b^{(k)}
\right),
$$

where $\phi$ is $\tanh$ in the original paper (or ReLU
in modern reimplementations) and the kernel $W^{(k)}$ is
shared across all spatial positions $(i, j)$.  Despite its
small size by today's standards (≈60 k parameters), LeNet
established the convolutional template — conv → nonlinearity →
pool → repeat → flatten → MLP — that is still recognisable in
every CNN that followed.
